channel.title: State-Policy Think-Tank Digest
channel.description: Latest posts from free-market policy institutes across the U.S.

item.title: [Foropportunity] Strengthening families for generational change: GCO welcomes Travis Thorne to the team
item.description: The post Strengthening families for generational change: GCO welcomes Travis Thorne to the team appeared first on Georgia Center For Opportunity.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] RGF talks to KOAT TV about roads
item.description:
SB 2 has passed which means that the road issue has been “put to bed” for the time being. The bill which has been signed by the Gov. includes: $1.5 billion in new road spending via a bond (debt) package. This also includes: A 35% increase in the weight-distance tax on trucks A 25% increase […]

The post RGF talks to KOAT TV about roads appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.title: [Mainepolicy] An Open Letter to Maine’s Legislature: Stop Advancing a Bill You Know is Unconstitutional
item.description:
Last June, Maine Policy Institute urged Governor Janet Mills to veto LD 1666, a bill expanding ranked-choice voting (RCV) to elections for governor, state senator, and state representative. That request was grounded in clear constitutional text, the Maine Supreme Judicial Court’s 2017 advisory opinion, and Governor Mills’ own legal analysis as Attorney General. Since then, […]

The post An Open Letter to Maine’s Legislature: Stop Advancing a Bill You Know is Unconstitutional appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Cascadepolicy] Portland’s “Affordable” Housing Averages $800 per Square Foot
item.description:
By Randal O’Toole Note: This post is a sequel to Metro’s Plan for...

The post Portland’s “Affordable” Housing Averages $800 per Square Foot appeared first on Cascade Policy Institute.

item.title: [Rifreedom] Center Launches K-12 Student Video Contest to Celebrate America’s 250th Birthday #America250RI
item.description:
In recognition of President's Day and George Washington's upcoming birthday, the RI Center for Freedom & Prosperity today launched a video contest encouraging K-12 students to create a video about why they are proud to celebrate America's semiquincentennial, commemorating the 250th Anniversary of the US Declaration of Independence.

The post Center Launches K-12 Student Video Contest to Celebrate America’s 250th Birthday #America250RI appeared first on RI Center for Freedom and Prosperity.

item.title: [Cardinalinstitute] West Virginia’s hospital debate is missing a key piece of the financial picture
item.description:
West Virginia’s hospital debate is missing a key piece of the financial picture Debates over hospital policy in West Virginia usually follow a familiar pattern. Hospital systems warn that their finances are weak. Industry groups say they have too many patients with government insurance. Certificate of Need (CON) rules are defended as needed to keep […]

The post West Virginia’s hospital debate is missing a key piece of the financial picture appeared first on Cardinal Institute.

item.title: [Beacontn] We Can’t Afford to Trim Your Trees, but We Can Hire Lobbyists
item.description: We’ve long called for reform to the practice of taxpayer-funded lobbying. But this NES response is probably the most glaring example of the need for reform.

item.title: [Reforminggovernment] The Wisconsin Administrative Reform Toolbox
item.description:
AdministrativeReformToolbox

The post The Wisconsin Administrative Reform Toolbox appeared first on Institute for Reforming Government.

item.title: [Reforminggovernment] IRG Releases “Wisconsin Administrative Reform Toolbox” as Playbook for States Seeking to Replicate Wisconsin’s Model
item.description:
IRG Releases “Wisconsin Administrative Reform Toolbox” as Playbook for States Seeking to Replicate Wisconsin’s Model Delafield, Wis. – The Institute for Reforming Government (IRG) released its “Wisconsin Administrative Reform Toolbox” on Monday, a practical handbook designed to help other states replicate Wisconsin’s reform framework. WHY IT MATTERS: In the 2010s, Wisconsin led one of the […]

The post IRG Releases “Wisconsin Administrative Reform Toolbox” as Playbook for States Seeking to Replicate Wisconsin’s Model appeared first on Institute for Reforming Government.

item.title: [Mspolicy] Introducing Mississippi Momentum: A Blueprint for Lasting Prosperity
item.description:
Too many young people still leave Mississippi to chase opportunities elsewhere.  MCPP is on a mission to help change that - by creating the conditions for real, sustained growth so our children and grandchildren choose to stay, build lives, and thrive right here in our state. The good news?  Mississippi is no longer a laggard, but leading. […]

The post Introducing Mississippi Momentum: A Blueprint for Lasting Prosperity appeared first on Mississippi Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Mainepolicy] Ending the Sales Tax on Electricity
item.description:
Maine lawmakers are currently considering a proposal that would exempt all residential electricity from the state sales tax. While it may be a relatively small cut among all of Maine’s over-taxation and regulation, it is nevertheless good policy as well as an important acknowledgment of the growing reality that electricity costs are putting real pressure […]

The post Ending the Sales Tax on Electricity appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] RGF Freedom Index Results Finalized
item.description:
As we have done for more than a decade, the Rio Grande Foundation has tracked the votes in New Mexico’s legislative sessions. The 2026 session was a short, 30-day legislative session, but some momentous bills passed (for better AND worse) and some horrible bills that could have passed were defeated, but often included floor votes […]

The post RGF Freedom Index Results Finalized appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.title: [Libertas] Three New Board Members Join Libertas Network
item.description:
Libertas Network is growing. As we expand our national programs to serve millions of families and spread the ideas of human flourishing, we need leaders who match the scale of that ambition. That’s why we’re excited to announce the addition of three extraordinary individuals to our board of trustees. Each one brings deep experience, bold […]

The post Three New Board Members Join Libertas Network appeared first on Libertas Network: Changing Hearts, Minds, and Laws.

item.title: [Newjerseypolicyinstitute] Beyond the Fiscal Time Bomb: 5 Ways New Jersey Can Cut the Bloat and Empower Families This Year
item.description:
by Erica Jedynak, NJPI Board Member New Jersey is currently sitting on a fiscal time bomb. Despite record-shattering $58 billion […]

The post Beyond the Fiscal Time Bomb: 5 Ways New Jersey Can Cut the Bloat and Empower Families This Year first appeared on New Jersey Policy Institute.

item.title: [Arkansaspolicyfoundation] SOLAR, WIND & ARKANSAS MOTOR VEHICLES
item.description: (February 2026) Arkansans are heavily dependent on private transportation. The Bureau of Transportation Statistics (BTS) reports that 93% of Arkansans reach work by private motor vehicles, while only 0.4% use public transportation. The 102,622 miles of roads in Arkansas dwarf the 2,505 miles of freight railroad and 1,860 miles of waterway cited by the BTS, […]

item.title: [Mspolicy] Dispatch from the Dome: Strengthening Private Support for Mississippi Families
item.description:
This week at the Capitol has been quieter on major headline legislation, but that doesn’t mean meaningful policy conversations aren’t happening. One bill worth highlighting right now is HB 1944 — a proposal that focuses on strengthening private support for Mississippi families and nonprofit organizations serving vulnerable communities. Here’s what it does — and why […]

The post Dispatch from the Dome: Strengthening Private Support for Mississippi Families appeared first on Mississippi Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] Kansas Should Reject Price Controls
item.description: Kansas families feel the squeeze. Prescription drugs are expensive. Credit card balances are heavy. Rents and groceries are up. When people are hurting, it’s understandable that lawmakers reach for a simple-sounding fix: cap the price. But that’s where good intentions collide with bad economics. Milton Friedman’s warning applies perfectly here: don’t judge policy by its […]

item.title: [Badgerinstitute] Ethnic studies courses required to graduate at all 13 four-year UW schools
item.description:
All 13 four-year University of Wisconsin System schools impose an ethnic studies requirement in order to graduate.

item.title: [Badgerinstitute] Grades now hyper-inflated at UW-Madison
item.description:
The average GPA for undergraduate students at Wisconsin’s flagship university increased to 3.48 in the recently completed fall semester — up from 3.28 just 10 years ago.

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] Freedom Foundation research cited in new Trump rule
item.description:
The Trump administration earlier this month issued a final rule to hold federal bureaucrats more accountable to the will of American voters, the culmination of a months-long review process after the administration’s Office of Personnel Management (OPM) first proposed the regulatory reforms last spring.

item.title: [Platteinstitute] Childcare Freedom in Nebraska
item.description:
In today’s economy, the high cost and low availability of childcare are major barriers for working families. While policymakers often look toward taxpayer-funded subsidies to solve the problem, recent research suggests a different culprit: state-level overregulation.  Nebraska stands at a critical juncture in this national conversation. According to the 2024 State Childcare Regulations Index from...

The post Childcare Freedom in Nebraska appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Empowerms] Empower MS Releases 2025 Impact Report
item.description:
As we look back on 2025, we are reminded how much can change when Mississippians come together around a simple conviction: every person, no matter their ZIP code, income, or background, deserves the opportunity to rise. Last year brought meaningful progress across education, work, and justice. Mississippi continued to earn national attention for its academic…

The post Empower MS Releases 2025 Impact Report first appeared on Empower Mississippi.

item.description:

item.title: [Platteinstitute] LB 663: Nebraska Agricultural Zoning Reform Improves County Permitting Process
item.description:
On February 25, 2026, Governor Jim Pillen signed Legislative Bill 663 into law while at the Nebraska Dairy Convention and Trade Show in West Point, Nebraska. Introduced by Senator Tanya Storer, and championed by The Platte Institute, LB 663 improves county zoning processes for agricultural producers by establishing a more uniform, transparent, and timely system...

item.title: [Empowerms] Dr. Clay Routledge: Meaning, Purpose, and Flourishing
item.description:
Dr. Clay Routledge: Meaning, Purpose, and Flourishing In this episode of The Empower Podcast, host Grant Callen sits down with Dr. Clay Routledge, Executive Vice President of the Archbridge Institute and Director of the Human Flourishing Lab, to explore what really makes life meaningful and why that matters for public policy in Mississippi and beyond.…

item.title: [Beacontn] Why Tennessee Doesn’t Need a State Property Tax
item.description: We’ve made it just fine for more than 75 years without a state property tax, and we’ll be just fine in the future without one.

item.description:

The passage of medical malpractice reform is the most important public policy success in New Mexico in more than a decade. It took support from New Mexicans of every political stripe and a bipartisan coalition in the Legislature to […]

item.title: [Alabamapolicy] Alabama Needs to Cut the Red Tape for Rural Hospitals
item.description:
Alabama’s rural hospitals are struggling, and our state’s bureaucratic red tape isn’t helping. The Alabama Hospital Association reports 27 hospitals in danger of closing and 19 in immediate risk.

item.title: [Alabamapolicy] Alabama’s most influential political groups converge as ‘Freedom First Alabama’ to wage war on big government policies
item.description:

item.title: [Will Law] Choice and Charter Schools Outperform Public Schools, New Report Shows
item.description:
Students attending Wisconsin’s private choice and charter schools outperform their peers in traditional public schools in both English Language Arts (ELA) and math, even after accounting for demographic differences, according to a new Apples to Apples report released by WILL.

The post Choice and Charter Schools Outperform Public Schools, New Report Shows appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] Why Kansas Wins When SCOTUS Stops Tariff Overreach
item.description: Kansas cannot treat trade as a talking point. Trade is a pillar of the state’s economy, from Wichita manufacturing to rural agriculture. item.description: The number of states eliminating or reforming their requirements to braid natural hair has increased over the last few years. Today, 37 states no longer require a permit to braid hair, freeing entrepreneurs to earn a living using a skill that is often passed down through generations of women. Unfortunately, Louisiana loves to buck a...

item.description:
Yesterday marked one of the most important procedural deadlines of the legislative session. It was the deadline for committees to report general bills and constitutional amendments originating in the other chamber. While it may sound technical, this deadline has a very real impact on which policies still have a chance of becoming law this year. Here’s […]

The second segment, however, returned to a staple theme of the CBS news team. It presented...

The post Amend apartment floor area ratios to increase housing density appeared first on Grassroot Institute of Hawaii.

item.title: [Itrfoundation] Low Turnout, High Approval: 11 of 12 School Tax Measures Pass in March Special Election
item.description:
Turnout in the March special election remained extremely low across the participating districts. Across the 12 contests, just 6,336 votes were cast out of 136,901 registered voters — a turnout of only 4.6 percent. Individual district turnout varied significantly. The highest turnout occurred in Sibley-Ocheyedan CSD, where 34.8 percent of registered voters participated in the PPEL renewal election. The lowest turnout occurred in Maquoketa CSD, where just 2.1 percent of registered voters cast ballots. Even in districts where turnout was higher than expected — such as South Winneshiek and Decorah CSDs — participation remained well below the levels seen during recent bond elections, where turnout reached 48.6 percent and 73 percent respectively. While nearly all measures passed, the extremely low turnout means that long-term tax decisions affecting multiple school districts were determined by only a small fraction of eligible voters.

The post Low Turnout, High Approval: 11 of 12 School Tax Measures Pass in March Special Election appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] Beyond the Yard Signs: Looking for Principled Policy in Louisiana Candidates
item.description: Numerous political races are currently underway in Louisiana, which can be both exciting and overwhelming as voters attempt to stay informed. Beyond the labels, slogans, and talking points lie real policy issues in need of principled solutions. As Louisiana heads into the 2026 Public Service Commission race, energy and infrastructure policy will be front and...

item.description:
Wisconsin’s public school teachers are leaving their classrooms at higher rates than they have in more than 25 years. Here, we present their voices.

item.title: [Will Law] WILL Praises Department of Education for Title IX Investigation into New Richmond Schools, Encourages Further Action in Westosha and Sun Prairie
item.description:
WILL has issued a statement in response to the Department of Education's announcement of a Title IX investigation into the New Richmond School District.

item.title: [Itrfoundation] When the Tax Code Becomes a Policy Weapon
item.description:
Some states are funding rising spending with wealth taxes and targeted levies instead of restraining government growth. This marks a shift away from broad, neutral tax policy. A growing divide is emerging between states expanding government and those prioritizing tax competitiveness. Policy choices today will shape migration, investment, and job growth tomorrow. Targeted taxes, including those on firearms, highlight the risk of using the tax code to steer behavior or place financial pressure on constitutional rights. The implications extend beyond revenue to fundamental freedoms.

The post When the Tax Code Becomes a Policy Weapon appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Mackinac] ‘All of your data has been stolen’
item.description: Who's stealing your tax dollars? Haywood Talcove has the answer, and it's worse than you think

item.title: [Inpolicy] The Outstater
item.description: Delineating ‘Growth OUR MAYOR likes to tout her city as being one of the fastest “growing’ in the state. The scare quotes are to alert you that growth needs some definition. The mayor is talking demographically, that is, her city is increasing in the raw number of people. That is the wrong metric, it will be argued Read the full article...

In December, a federal district court in Southern California issued a statewide permanent injunction in Mirabelli...

item.title: [Texaspolicy] Assessing Tax Trends in Fort Bend County
item.description:
Does history suggest that property taxes are too high in Fort Bend County? And, if so, have local decision-makers actively helped or hurt the situation?   To help answer these questions, let’s review the county’s 2024 Annual Comprehensive Financial Report (ACFR) and gather four types of data—i.e., tax levies, local population, total tax rates, and taxable values—over a 10-year time...

The post Assessing Tax Trends in Fort Bend County first appeared on Texas Public Policy Foundation.

item.title: [Inpolicy] Franke: Immigration
item.description: by Mark Franke Political drama. Sadly, this was the reluctant opinion of my Socratic discussion group after we took on the issue of immigration last month. Any workable long-term solution requires political sense in Washington DC and particularly in the halls of Congress. Expecting anything sensible from that dysfunctional bunch is drawing into extremely long Read the full article...

item.title: [Itrfoundation] Iowa Shows How to Put Parents Back in Charge
item.description:
Iowa is the first state approved for a federal “Returning Education to the States” waiver, allowing it to consolidate several federal programs into a $9.5 million block grant and giving the state greater flexibility in how education funds are used. The move reflects a broader shift away from federal micromanagement toward state-led education policy, aligning with long-standing conservative arguments that states and parents—not Washington—should have the primary role in education decisions. Combined with Iowa’s recent reforms expanding Education Savings Accounts, charter schools, and open enrollment, the waiver strengthens the state’s push toward greater parental choice and student-centered education funding.

The post Iowa Shows How to Put Parents Back in Charge appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Texaspolicy] Assessing Tax Trends in Hidalgo County
item.description:
Does history suggest that property taxes are too high in Hidalgo County? And, if so, have local decision-makers actively helped or hurt the situation?   To help answer these questions, let’s review the county’s 2023 Annual Comprehensive Financial Report (ACFR) and gather four types of data—i.e., tax levies, local population, total tax rates, and taxable values—over a 10-year time horizon. Using...

item.title: [Pacificresearch] Trump’s Plan To Peg Drug Prices to Foreign Countries Could Backfire
item.description:
President Trump famously quipped during his run for a second term that he had “concepts of a plan” to fix health care. That plan finally came together in January — and congressional Republicans are eager to codify its concepts into law. Most of Mr. Trump’s “Great Healthcare Plan” is promising. It would make more medicines […]

item.title: [Jbartlett] ‘Executive compensation’ regulation would hurt North Country hospitals
item.description:
New Hampshire has been lucky. Since 2005, 110 rural hospitals in the United States have closed, and another 85 have converted to an out-patient service provider. Maine was home to three of those conversions and one closure, all since 2013.  No closure or conversion has hit New Hampshire. Yet.  That could change if the state […]

The post ‘Executive compensation’ regulation would hurt North Country hospitals appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] Bipartisan effort to kill property tax relief based on false claims, innuendo, and allegiance to local government
item.description: Virtually every member of the Kansas Legislature says they support property tax relief, but there is a bipartisan effort behind the scenes to kill it. The reasons vary, and some are unspoken (allegiance to local government, the proposed legislation isn’t their idea, etc.), and the objections are mostly based on false or unproven claims. The […]

item.title: [Mackinac] Michigan taxpayers build it, but nobody comes
item.description: Businesses should pay for their own land

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] Idaho Senate approves statewide framework for short-term rentals
item.description: HB 583 establishes a clear statewide standard for short-term rentals (STRs), ensuring that local governments cannot impose arbitrary restrictions that effectively prohibit homeowners from renting their property.

item.title: [Johnlocke] Trump still has time for turnaround
item.description:
Eddie Scarry writes for the Federalist about President Donald Trump’s political fortune. It’s only a little more than a month into the second year of President Trump’s second term, but it’s turning out to be a MAGA disappointment. Fortunately, there’s still time ahead of the November midterm for Trump to get his administration back on...

The post Trump still has time for turnaround appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Johnlocke] Jackson’s son blasts Obama, Biden for politicizing memorial
item.description:
Anna Young writes for the New York Post about an interesting response to the Jesse Jackson memorial. Rev. Jesse Jackson’s son ripped former Presidents Joe Biden, Barack Obama and Bill Clinton for using the late civil rights icon’s memorial service to take shots at President Trump. Jesse Jackson Jr. said the grandstanding Democrats didn’t really...

The post Jackson’s son blasts Obama, Biden for politicizing memorial appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Mackinac] Right to compute moves forward in other states
item.description: States turning away from heavy-handed AI regulation laws

item.title: [Pacificresearch] Another Tax That Backfired
item.description:
Passed in 2022 by nearly 58% of Angeleno voters, Measure ULA initially imposes an additional 4% tax on the sales of any homes or commercial properties, not just mansions, valued at more than $5.3 million. The rate on sales of properties sold at more than $10.6 million rises to 5.5%. The dollars coming in are dedicated to funding affordable housing projects and providing […]

The post Another Tax That Backfired appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.

item.title: [Illinoispolicy] Record-high Illinois university workers opt-out of pensions
item.description:
A record share of Illinois university employees opt-out of pensions for a 401(k)-style plan, lawmakers should give other state employees the same flexibility.

The post Record-high Illinois university workers opt-out of pensions appeared first on Illinois Policy.

item.title: [Illinoispolicy] Here’s who CTU supports in the 2026 primary elections
item.description:
The Chicago Teachers Union shared its 2026 state primary election candidate endorsements.

The post Here’s who CTU supports in the 2026 primary elections appeared first on Illinois Policy.

item.title: [Texaspolicy] Assessing Tax Trends in El Paso County
item.description:
Does history suggest that property taxes are too high in El Paso County? And, if so, have local decision-makers actively helped or hurt the situation?   To help answer these questions, let’s review the county’s 2024 Annual Comprehensive Financial Report (ACFR) and gather four types of data—i.e., tax levies, local population, total tax rates, and taxable values—over a 10-year time...

The post Assessing Tax Trends in El Paso County first appeared on Texas Public Policy Foundation.

item.title: [Will Law] Racial Achievement Gaps in Wisconsin Explained by Poverty and Family Structure, New Report Shows
item.description:
A new report from WILL examines why Wisconsin has some of the largest academic achievement gaps between Black and white students in the nation—and finds that poverty, family structure, and early literacy explain much of the disparity.

The post Racial Achievement Gaps in Wisconsin Explained by Poverty and Family Structure, New Report Shows appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Inpolicy] The Outstater
item.description: A Mamdani for Indianapolis DIFFERENTATION is not allowed in the corporate newsroom. That is, all individuals and groups are to be treated equal in their motivation and character (except conservatives, who are racist and evil). This softens intra-office relationships but makes predicting the twists and turns of politics difficult.  So it is no surprise that nobody is asking this simple question: In Indianapolis, could Read the full article...

item.title: [Jbartlett] Simple charts can empower and inform local voters
item.description:
New Hampshire’s cherished tradition of self-government has become eroded by the ever-increasing complexity of modern government. It is eroded further by the practice of sharing only very limited information with voters before and during local elections. A house bill this session would arrest this erosion by empowering voters with detailed but easy-to-understand information about their […]

The post Simple charts can empower and inform local voters appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Reforminggovernment] IRG Releases School Referenda Transparency Tool for Taxpayers
item.description:
IRG Releases School Referenda Transparency Tool for Taxpayers New resource helps Wisconsin voters understand local school referenda Delafield, Wis. – The Institute for Reforming Government (IRG) released an online school referendum transparency tool on Tuesday to help taxpayers better understand school spending ahead of spring 2026 school district referenda decisions. The tool is available on […]

The post IRG Releases School Referenda Transparency Tool for Taxpayers appeared first on Institute for Reforming Government.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] As Public School Enrollment Declines, Study Looks to Voc-Techs for Lessons on Regionalization
item.description:
State workforce is growing; continuing to meet state economy’s talent needs will likely require smaller school districts to regionalize   BOSTON – With the number of K-12 public school students consistently declining as the size of the state workforce continues to grow,...

The post As Public School Enrollment Declines, Study Looks to Voc-Techs for Lessons on Regionalization appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.title: [Mainepolicy] LDs 229 + 1089: Higher Taxes. Slower Growth. Fewer Jobs.
item.description:
Politicians in Augusta look poised to pass LDs 229 and 1089 this session, which would raise Maine’s top income tax rate to 10.95%. However, our research concludes that either one of these bills could individually cause serious damage to Maine’s economy if passed. If both bills were passed, it would stack multiple tax increases on […]

The post LDs 229 + 1089: Higher Taxes. Slower Growth. Fewer Jobs. appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Illinoispolicy] Illinois’ population loss could make congressional gerrymandering harder in 2030
item.description:
The state is predicted to lose a U.S. House seat in the next redistricting cycle.

The post Illinois’ population loss could make congressional gerrymandering harder in 2030 appeared first on Illinois Policy.

item.title: [Grassrootinstitute] Fighting to save Hawaii’s income tax cuts
item.description:
I wish more of our lawmakers would realize that no government program could do as much to help struggling families in the islands as significant tax cuts.  But maybe public reaction to the governor’s effort to “pause” the phase-in of the state’s historic 2024 income tax cuts will help them come to their senses. Lawmakers […]

The post Fighting to save Hawaii’s income tax cuts appeared first on Grassroot Institute of Hawaii.

item.title: [Jamesmadison] Levy Caps for Property Tax Reform in Florida
item.description:
Introduction Florida’s current property tax system places a growing financial burden on homeowners, renters, and employers across the...

The post Levy Caps for Property Tax Reform in Florida appeared first on James Madison Institute.

item.title: [Goldwaterinstitute] How the Ronald Reagan Fellowship Helps Launch Careers in the Freedom Movement
item.description:
For students and young professionals who want to build a career advancing liberty, public policy, and constitutional principles, the Goldwater..

The post How the Ronald Reagan Fellowship Helps Launch Careers in the Freedom Movement appeared first on Goldwater Institute.

item.title: [Johnlocke] Stein announces top spending priorities before the fiscal year ends
item.description:
Stein has declared the Medicaid shortfall urgent and proposed a “Critical Needs Budget” for FY 2025–26 to address “funding needs that cannot wait until next year.” The proposal primarily focuses on funding the Medicaid rebase and increasing compensation for teachers and other state employees.

The post Stein announces top spending priorities before the fiscal year ends appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Idahofreedom] House Bill 845 — Medicaid, presumptive eligibility (+1)
item.description: Bill Description: House Bill 845 would empower the Department of Health and Welfare to discipline hospitals who abuse Medicaid presumptive eligibility. Rating: +1 Does it increase government spending (for objectionable purposes) or debt? Conversely, does it decrease government spending or debt? The Medicaid presumptive eligibility program allows hospitals to provide services to uninsured patients by […]

item.title: [Americanexperiment] UNCOVERED: The Origin Story of Fraud in Minnesota
item.description:
Welcome back! This week on the American Experiment Podcast… This week, Kathryn and Bill kick things off with the DFL’s new proposal to tax ICE agents who worked in Minnesota. One Republican asked whether that tax would also apply to paid protesters, something the DFL didn’t seem to appreciate, despite their well-known love of higher...

The post UNCOVERED: The Origin Story of Fraud in Minnesota appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] We can't be afraid to talk about the major Medicaid budget problem
item.description: Supporters of the current system often emphasize that the federal government pays the majority of the cost. That is true. But it misses a key point: the state share still approaches $1 billion annually, and it grows as the program expands.

item.title: [Pacificresearch] Newsom oversees years of failed California water policy
item.description:
2026 began in California with two atmospheric rivers and storms, which resulted in precipitation that was 119% of average rainfall for January. However, much of it flowed into the Pacific Ocean. Moreover, by the end of February our statewide snowpack was only 66% of average for this date, constituting a significant decline from 85% at […]

The post Newsom oversees years of failed California water policy appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.

item.title: [Idahofreedom] House Bill 809 — Child protection, school of origin (+3)
item.description: Bill Description: House Bill 809 (H809) amends Idaho’s Child Protective Act to include provisions recognizing “school of origin” for a child involved in a protective services case. Additionally, it adds a new section which establishes a corresponding school of origin hearing process.      Rating: +3 Does it violate the spirit or the letter of either the […]

item.title: [Newjerseypolicyinstitute] New Jersey Policy Institute Releases First “Pork Roll Report” as Governor Proposes FY2027 Budget
item.description:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE March 10, 2026 Trenton, NJ — As Governor Mikie Sherrill today proposed a $60.7 billion budget for Fiscal […]

The post New Jersey Policy Institute Releases First “Pork Roll Report” as Governor Proposes FY2027 Budget first appeared on New Jersey Policy Institute.

item.title: [Jbartlett] NH could be the first state to let non-union public employees negotiate their contracts
item.description:
New Hampshire led the American colonies 250 years ago by adopt a constitution that created an independent government before anyone else. So it’s fitting that in 2026 New Hampshire could achieve another first for independence. Like all Americans, government employees have the constitutional right to free speech and free association. In 2018, the U.S. Supreme […]

The post NH could be the first state to let non-union public employees negotiate their contracts appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Americanexperiment] New bill proposes a constitutional sales tax hike to fund “affordability”
item.description:
Disregarding the 1 percent sales tax hike already enacted by the 2023 legislature, a new bill, SF 2621, and its House companion, HF 3279, will ask voters to amend the state constitution and further raise the rate by “three-eighths of one percent” between 2027 and 2052. As the ballot phrasing explains, the proceeds will fund...

The post New bill proposes a constitutional sales tax hike to fund “affordability” appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] Report Overview: Creating Opportunities to Bring Seawater Desalination to Coastal Federal Lands in California
item.description:
Executive Summary California’s water supply challenges increasingly pose risks not only to the state’s economy, but to national food production, interstate commerce, international trade, and national economic stability and security. While seawater desalination is a proven technology used worldwide to expand reliable water supplies, large-scale projects along California’s coast have faced significant regulatory barriers at...

The post Report Overview: Creating Opportunities to Bring Seawater Desalination to Coastal Federal Lands in California first appeared on California Policy Center.

item.title: [Idahofreedom] House Bill 811 — Community infrastructure districts (-5)
item.description: Bill Description: House Bill 811 would allow counties to create community infrastructure districts and impose special assessments (property taxes) on property owners without direct voter approval. It would also create a new fee for property owners. Rating: -5 NOTE: House Bill 811 is related to House Bill 437 (2026) and House Bill 407 (2025). Does it create, expand, or […]

item.title: [Americanexperiment] Governor Walz’s Medicaid Centralization Plan Will Just Make Things Worse:
item.description:
Simply hiding the entire statewide Medicaid system under a governor-controlled bureau in Saint Paul, driven by a new website, will just make things worse.

The post Governor Walz’s Medicaid Centralization Plan Will Just Make Things Worse: appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] Washington state is blowing up its no-income-tax advantage
item.description: With one single vote, lawmakers have decided to defy legal precedent, blow up the state’s business climate, and deny citizens their right of referendum. A trifecta of bad policy that firmly signals “Choose Washington” is a slogan and strategy of the past.
